cours:
CHAPTER 1 — GRAMMAR FOUNDATIONS (A2)

1. PRESENT SIMPLE vs PRESENT CONTINUOUS

The present simple is used to describe habitual actions, permanent states, and general truths. It is the default tense for things that happen regularly or are always true. Affirmative: I work / she works / they work. Negative: I do not work / she does not work. Interrogative: Do you work? / Does she work?
The present continuous (present progressive) describes an action happening right now or around the time of speaking. It is formed with: subject + am/is/are + verb-ing. Affirmative: I am working / she is configuring / they are testing. Negative: I am not working / she is not running the script. Interrogative: Are you working? / Is the server running?
IT examples: 'The firewall blocks all unauthorized traffic.' (present simple — always true) vs 'The firewall is blocking a suspicious IP right now.' (present continuous — happening now).

2. PAST SIMPLE vs PAST CONTINUOUS

The past simple describes a completed action at a specific time in the past. Regular verbs add -ed: I worked, I configured, I installed. Irregular verbs change form: I went, I found, I wrote, I ran. Negative: I did not work / she did not install. Interrogative: Did you configure the router? Did she run the backup?
The past continuous describes an action that was in progress at a specific moment in the past. It is often used with the past simple to show an interruption: 'I was configuring the switch when the power went out.' Formation: subject + was/were + verb-ing.
IT example: 'The technician was monitoring the logs when the alert appeared.'

3. QUESTION FORMATION

Yes/No questions use an auxiliary verb before the subject. Present: Do you manage the servers? / Does the DHCP server assign IPs automatically? Past: Did the backup complete successfully? Did you patch the vulnerability?
WH questions use a question word + auxiliary + subject + verb. Where do you store the backups? When did the incident occur? How does the VPN work? Who configured the firewall? Why did the server crash?

4. BASIC SENTENCE STRUCTURE (SVO)

English follows Subject + Verb + Object order. 'The technician installs the server.' (S=technician, V=installs, O=server). 'The administrator created a new user account.' 'The router forwards packets to the correct network.' Adhering to SVO order is essential; unlike French, subject and verb do not invert in statements.

5. MODAL VERBS

Can expresses ability: 'I can configure VLANs on this switch.' Could expresses past ability or a polite request: 'Could you check the logs?' / 'The old server could handle 100 users.' Should expresses advice or recommendation: 'You should update the firmware regularly.' Must expresses strong obligation or logical deduction: 'You must back up your data daily.' / 'The server must be overloaded — it is very slow.' Will expresses future actions or promises: 'I will deploy the patch tonight.' Would expresses conditional or polite requests: 'Would you restart the service?' / 'That would improve performance.'

6. ARTICLES: A / AN / THE / ZERO ARTICLE

Use a/an for indefinite reference, especially the first time you mention something. A before consonant sounds: a server, a firewall, a user. An before vowel sounds: an error, an IP address, an update. Use the for specific, previously mentioned, or unique items: 'Please restart the server' (you both know which one). Zero article for general statements: 'Routers forward packets.' / 'Hackers exploit vulnerabilities.'

7. PREPOSITIONS OF TIME AND PLACE

Time: at for specific times (at 9am, at midnight), on for days and dates (on Monday, on 15 March), in for months, years, periods (in July, in 2024, in the morning). Place: in for enclosed spaces (in the server room, in the cloud), on for surfaces or OS (on the desk, on Windows), at for specific points or locations (at the office, at the data centre).

8. COMPARATIVES AND SUPERLATIVES

Short adjectives add -er / -est: fast → faster → the fastest; big → bigger → the biggest. Long adjectives use more / the most: complex → more complex → the most complex; secure → more secure → the most secure. Irregular: good → better → the best; bad → worse → the worst. IT examples: 'Fibre is faster than ADSL.' / 'This firewall is more reliable than the previous one.' / 'The most critical vulnerability must be patched first.'

notions_cles.definition: Tense for habitual actions and general truths (I work, she works)
Tense for actions happening right now, formed with am/is/are + verb-ing (I am working)
Tense for completed past actions (I configured, she installed)
Tense for ongoing past actions, often interrupted (I was monitoring when...)
Auxiliary expressing ability (can), advice (should), obligation (must), future (will)
Subject + Verb + Object — the standard English sentence order
Used before specific or previously mentioned nouns (the server, the firewall)
Used before non-specific nouns mentioned for the first time (a router, an error)
Form comparing two things: faster than, more secure than
Form expressing the highest degree: the fastest, the most complex
at (time), on (day/date), in (month/year/period)
Helper verb used to form questions and negatives in present/past simple

exemple_concret: IT support situation: 'The technician is configuring the new switch.' (present continuous — right now). 'Every morning, the monitoring tool checks all services.' (present simple — habitual). 'Yesterday, the administrator patched the vulnerability.' (past simple — completed). 'She was running the backup when the power cut occurred.' (past continuous interrupted by past simple). 'You must update the firmware and you should document every change.' (modals).

points_cles: Present simple = habit/truth; present continuous = now (am/is/are + -ing)
Past simple = finished action; past continuous = action in progress when interrupted
Questions: auxiliary (do/does/did) + subject + verb
Modals: can (ability), should (advice), must (obligation/deduction), will (future)
Articles: a/an (first mention, indefinite), the (specific), zero article (general)
Comparatives: -er than / more … than; superlatives: the -est / the most …
SVO word order is obligatory in English statements
formules: Present simple: S + V(s) | Do/Does + S + V? | S + do/does + not + V
Present continuous: S + am/is/are + V-ing | Am/Is/Are + S + V-ing?
Past simple: S + V-ed/irregular | Did + S + V? | S + did not + V
Past continuous: S + was/were + V-ing
Comparative short: adj + -er + than | Comparative long: more + adj + than
Superlative short: the + adj + -est | Superlative long: the most + adj

cours:
CHAPTER 2 — ESSENTIAL IT VOCABULARY (A2-B1)

Mastering IT vocabulary in English is fundamental for any BTS SIO SISR student. Below are key domains of vocabulary you need to know.

NETWORKING TERMS
A firewall is a network security device that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing traffic based on rules. A router is a device that routes data packets between different networks. A switch connects multiple devices within a LAN (Local Area Network) and forwards frames using MAC addresses. A VLAN (Virtual Local Area Network) is a logical network segmentation at layer 2. A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates an encrypted tunnel over a public network. DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses. DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP addresses to devices on a network. An IP address is a unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network. A subnet mask defines which portion of an IP address represents the network and which the host. A gateway is a node that connects two different networks, typically a router.

INFRASTRUCTURE AND SERVICES
A server is a computer that provides services or resources to other computers (clients) on a network. Cloud computing refers to internet-based delivery of computing services such as storage, servers, and software. Virtualization is the process of creating a virtual (software-based) version of hardware, OS, or network resources. A backup is a copy of data made to protect against accidental loss or corruption. Bandwidth is the maximum data transfer capacity of a network connection. Latency is the delay experienced in data transmission across a network. A protocol is a set of rules that governs how data is communicated between devices. TCP/IP is the fundamental suite of communication protocols used on the internet. HTTP/HTTPS are the protocols used for web communication; HTTPS adds TLS encryption. SSH (Secure Shell) provides an encrypted channel for remote command-line access. RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) allows graphical remote access to Windows systems. A proxy server acts as an intermediary between clients and servers. A load balancer distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers.

SECURITY TERMS
Encryption is the process of encoding data so that only authorized parties can read it. Authentication is the process of verifying the identity of a user or system. Authorization is granting permissions to an authenticated user. A vulnerability is a weakness in a system that can be exploited by attackers. A patch is a software update that fixes security flaws or bugs. Malware is any software designed to damage or disrupt systems. Ransomware is a type of malware that encrypts files and demands a ransom for decryption. Phishing is a fraudulent attempt to steal credentials by impersonating legitimate entities. An antivirus is software that detects and removes malicious programs. Two-factor authentication (2FA) requires two forms of identity verification. A certificate is a digital document that verifies the identity of an entity. PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) is the framework for managing digital certificates.

WINDOWS/ACTIVE DIRECTORY
Active Directory is Microsoft's directory service that stores information about users, computers, and groups. A domain controller is a server that runs Active Directory and authenticates users. A GPO (Group Policy Object) is a set of rules enforced across computers in a domain. A log is a recorded sequence of events in a system, used for auditing and troubleshooting. An audit is a systematic review of IT systems to assess security and compliance. Compliance refers to adherence to laws, regulations, or standards.

ITSM AND SUPPORT
RAID is a storage technology that combines multiple disks for redundancy or performance. An SLA (Service Level Agreement) defines the expected level of service between a provider and client. RPO (Recovery Point Objective) defines the maximum acceptable amount of data loss. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) defines the maximum acceptable downtime after an incident. Disaster recovery is the process of restoring IT systems after a major incident. An incident is an unplanned interruption or reduction in quality of an IT service. A ticket is a record of a support request in an ITSM tool. Monitoring is the continuous observation of system performance and availability. BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) refers to the policy allowing employees to use personal devices for work. IoT (Internet of Things) refers to the network of physical objects connected to the internet. An API (Application Programming Interface) allows software applications to communicate. A container is an isolated software package containing code and its dependencies. Docker is the most popular containerization platform.

exemple_concret: During an IT audit, the administrator reviewed the firewall logs and discovered that a phishing campaign had attempted to compromise user credentials. The DHCP server had assigned an IP address to an unknown device, which triggered a monitoring alert. The team applied a patch to fix the vulnerability, enabled 2FA on Active Directory accounts, and updated the GPO to enforce stronger password policies.

cours:
CHAPTER 3 — WORKPLACE COMMUNICATION (B1)

1. PROFESSIONAL EMAILS

Professional emails in IT follow a clear structure. Opening phrases: 'Dear Mr Smith,' / 'Dear IT Team,' / 'To whom it may concern,' — always use a comma after the salutation. Introductory sentences: 'I am writing to inform you that...' / 'I hope this email finds you well.' / 'Further to our conversation this morning, I would like to...' / 'I am contacting you regarding the network incident of 14 March.' Body structure: (1) Context — explain why you are writing. (2) Information or request — state the main content clearly. (3) Action needed — what you expect the recipient to do. Closing phrases: 'Please do not hesitate to contact me should you have any questions.' / 'I look forward to hearing from you.' / 'Thank you for your time and assistance.' Sign-off: 'Best regards,' (neutral/professional), 'Yours sincerely,' (formal, named recipient), 'Kind regards,' (slightly warmer).

2. WRITING IT INCIDENT REPORTS

An IT incident report documents what happened during an outage or security event. Header: Date, incident reference (INC-2024-0312), severity level (P1 Critical / P2 High / P3 Medium / P4 Low). Description section: clearly state what happened, when it started, which systems were affected, and the business impact. 'On 12 March 2024 at 14:35 UTC, the primary web server became unresponsive, affecting all external users.' Resolution section: list the actions taken and the timeline. 'At 14:40, the on-call engineer was paged. At 15:02, the service was restored by restarting the Apache process.' Prevention section: describe measures implemented to prevent recurrence. 'Automatic restart scripts have been deployed and monitoring thresholds have been adjusted.'

3. MEETING VOCABULARY

Agenda: list of topics to be discussed at a meeting. Minutes: official written record of what was discussed and decided. Action points: specific tasks assigned to individuals with a deadline. AOB (Any Other Business): items not on the agenda, raised at the end. Chair: the person leading and facilitating the meeting. Quorum: minimum number of participants required to make decisions. Useful phrases: 'Shall we move to the next agenda item?' / 'Could you take the minutes?' / 'Who is responsible for this action point?' / 'Let us set a deadline of Friday.'

4. PHONE CALLS FOR IT SUPPORT

Answering: 'Good morning, IT Support, [Name] speaking. How can I help you?' User's opening: 'I am calling about a network connectivity issue.' / 'I cannot access my emails.' Clarifying: 'Could you describe the problem in more detail?' / 'When did this start?' / 'Are other users affected?' / 'Have you tried restarting your workstation?' Escalating: 'I am going to escalate your ticket to the network team.' / 'Your reference number is INC-2024-0415.' Closing: 'I will update you by end of day.' / 'Thank you for calling IT Support. Goodbye.'

5. FORMAL vs INFORMAL LANGUAGE

In professional IT contexts, formal language is expected in emails, reports, and documentation. Formal: 'I would like to request access to the production server.' Informal: 'Can I get access to the prod server?' Formal: 'The issue has been resolved and services have been restored.' Informal: 'It is fixed now.' Formal: 'I am unable to attend the meeting due to a prior commitment.' Informal: 'I cannot make it.' Rule: in professional written communication, always prefer formal register.

6. LINKING WORDS

Contrast: however, nevertheless, on the other hand, although, despite, in spite of. Example: 'The firewall was configured correctly; however, the intrusion still occurred.' Addition: furthermore, in addition, moreover, also, besides. Example: 'The VPN was slow. Furthermore, authentication was failing intermittently.' Cause/Result: because (cause), therefore/as a result/consequently (result), since, due to. Example: 'The server crashed because the disk was full. As a result, all services were unavailable.' Sequence: firstly, secondly, then, next, after that, subsequently, finally. Example: 'Firstly, identify the incident. Then, assess the impact. Finally, implement a fix.'

7. COMPLAINT HANDLING

Step 1 — Acknowledge: 'I understand your frustration and I am sorry for the inconvenience.' Step 2 — Apologize: 'I sincerely apologize for the disruption this has caused.' Step 3 — Explain: 'The issue was caused by a hardware failure in the primary storage unit.' Step 4 — Offer solution: 'We have restored services from backup and the system is now fully operational.' Step 5 — Follow up: 'I will personally follow up with you within 24 hours to confirm everything is working correctly.'

8. WRITING A PROFESSIONAL IT SUPPORT TICKET

Title: short, clear summary — 'Cannot print to network printer on Floor 3'. Priority: Critical (system down, all users affected) / High / Medium / Low. Description: include who is affected, what the problem is, when it started, and the business impact. Steps already tried: 'Restarted the print spooler service — issue persists. Reinstalled printer driver — no change.'

notions_cles.definition: Salutation → context → request/information → action needed → closing
Document recording what happened, when, impact, resolution, and prevention
Official written record of what was discussed and decided at a meeting
Specific task assigned to a person with a deadline during a meeting
List of topics to be covered at a meeting
Professional, polite language used in official written communication
however, nevertheless, on the other hand — connects contrasting ideas
furthermore, moreover, in addition — adds complementary information
therefore, as a result, consequently — expresses the consequence of an action
Five-step process: acknowledge, apologize, explain, offer solution, follow up
Transferring a ticket to a higher-level support team when the first-line cannot resolve it
Ticket classification: Critical / High / Medium / Low based on impact

exemple_concret: Email example: 'Dear Mr Johnson, I am writing to inform you that the network outage you reported (INC-2024-0218) has been resolved. The issue was caused by a misconfigured VLAN trunk port. The configuration has been corrected and services were restored at 16:45 today. Furthermore, we have implemented additional monitoring to prevent recurrence. Please do not hesitate to contact us if you experience any further issues. Kind regards, N. Boudjenah, IT Support Team.'

Incident report: header (date/ref/severity), description (what/when/impact), resolution (actions/timeline), prevention
Formal language is mandatory in professional written IT communication

cours:
CHAPTER 4 — READING COMPREHENSION (B1)

1. STRATEGIES FOR IT TEXTS

Skimming means reading quickly to get the general idea of a text. Focus on: the title, headings and subheadings, the first and last sentence of each paragraph, any bold or italic text, captions under images. Skimming tells you: what is the topic? what type of document is it? is it positive, negative, or neutral? Scanning means searching for specific information. You look for: dates, figures, names, technical terms, acronyms. Do not read every word — let your eyes move quickly until you spot what you need. In the BTS exam, always skim first (30 seconds), then scan for answers to specific questions.

2. UNDERSTANDING IT DOCUMENTATION

You do not need to know every word. Use these strategies: Context clues: the surrounding words often reveal the meaning of an unknown term. 'The technician configured the proxy, an intermediary server that filters web requests.' — you can infer the meaning. Cognates: many IT terms are almost identical in French and English. configuration → configuration, protocol → protocole, authentication → authentification, infrastructure → infrastructure, vulnerability → vulnérabilité. Technical acronyms: if you know the full form, you can often work out the context. DNS = Domain Name System (you know DNS from SISR, so you can understand any sentence using it).

3. READING ERROR MESSAGES AND LOGS

A system log entry typically contains: Timestamp (when): '2024-03-12 14:35:02' Severity level (how serious): ERROR (critical problem), WARN (potential issue), INFO (normal event), DEBUG (diagnostic detail). Source (where): the service or process generating the message. Event description (what): 'Connection timeout to database server at 192.168.1.10:3306'. Common HTTP status codes you must know: 200 OK (success), 301/302 Redirect, 400 Bad Request (client error), 401 Unauthorized (authentication required), 403 Forbidden (access denied), 404 Not Found (resource does not exist), 500 Internal Server Error (server-side fault).

4. UNDERSTANDING SECURITY BULLETINS

CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) number: uniquely identifies a known vulnerability (e.g., CVE-2023-44487). CVSS score: Common Vulnerability Scoring System, rated 0 to 10. 0-3.9: Low, 4-6.9: Medium, 7-8.9: High, 9-10: Critical. A security bulletin typically contains: CVE ID, description of the vulnerability, affected products and versions, CVSS score, remediation (patch, workaround, update). Example: 'CVE-2023-44487 (CVSS 7.5 High): HTTP/2 Rapid Reset Attack affecting NGINX before version 1.25.3. Recommendation: update to version 1.25.3 or apply the provided workaround configuration.'

5. WHITE PAPER STRUCTURE

White papers are authoritative documents used in IT to explain technologies or propose solutions. Typical structure: Executive summary (1 page): key points for decision-makers. Problem statement: describes the challenge being addressed. Solution: describes the proposed approach. Technical details: implementation, architecture, specifications. Conclusion: summarizes benefits and next steps. When reading a white paper in an exam, focus on the executive summary and conclusion first.

6. TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS

Network diagrams: identify device types (router, switch, firewall symbols), connections (WAN/LAN links), IP addressing, VLAN assignments. Configuration tables: read left-to-right, understand column headers. IP addressing tables typically contain: Device name, Interface, IP address, Subnet mask, Gateway.

7. EXAM COMPREHENSION STRATEGIES

Step 1: Identify the text type (article, report, email, security bulletin, documentation). Step 2: Read the questions before reading the text in detail — know what to look for. Step 3: Answer exactly what is asked. Do not write everything you know about the topic. Step 4: Support your answers with evidence from the text: 'According to the text...' / 'The author states that...' / 'In paragraph 2, it is mentioned that...' Step 5: Never leave a question blank — use partial information or inference if needed.

8. AUTHENTIC IT TEXT TYPES

RFC (Request for Comments): technical standards documents from the IETF, e.g., RFC 7540 defines HTTP/2. Vendor documentation: official guides from Cisco, Microsoft, etc. Use the table of contents and index. Security advisories: issued by CERT, CISA, or vendors like Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC). Job postings: read for required skills, certifications (CCNA, Security+), and tools.

notions_cles.definition: Reading quickly to get the general meaning — focus on titles, first sentences
Searching for specific information such as dates, names, figures
Surrounding words that help deduce the meaning of an unknown term
Word that looks similar in French and English (configuration, protocol, infrastructure)
A recorded system event containing timestamp, severity, source, and description
Classification of log events: ERROR, WARN, INFO, DEBUG
HTTP error code meaning the requested resource was not found
HTTP error code meaning an internal server error occurred
Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — unique identifier for a known security vulnerability
Numerical severity rating for a vulnerability from 0 (none) to 10 (critical)
Request for Comments — official technical standard document from the IETF
Authoritative document explaining a technology or proposing a solution
Brief overview of key points in a white paper aimed at decision-makers

exemple_concret: Exam technique: You receive a security bulletin. Step 1 — skim: it is about a critical vulnerability in OpenSSL. Step 2 — scan for CVE number (CVE-2022-0778), CVSS score (7.5 High), and affected versions (OpenSSL < 1.1.1n). Step 3 — answer: 'According to the bulletin, all systems running OpenSSL prior to version 1.1.1n are vulnerable. The recommended remediation is to update to version 1.1.1n or higher immediately.'

points_cles: Skim first (global understanding), then scan (specific answers)
Use context clues and cognates to understand unknown words
Log severity: ERROR > WARN > INFO > DEBUG
HTTP codes: 200=OK, 403=Forbidden, 404=Not Found, 500=Server Error
CVE = vulnerability ID; CVSS 0-10 = severity (9-10 = Critical)
Always support answers with text evidence: 'According to the text...'
Read exam questions BEFORE reading the text to know what to look for
formules: Log format: [timestamp] [LEVEL] [source] — event description
CVE format: CVE-YEAR-NUMBER (e.g., CVE-2023-44487)
White paper order: Executive Summary → Problem → Solution → Technical → Conclusion
Answer phrase: 'According to the text...' / 'The author states that...'

cours:
CHAPTER 5 — LISTENING & ORAL SKILLS (B1)

1. BTS ORAL PRESENTATION — COMPTE RENDU METHOD

The BTS oral exam requires you to present a document in English and answer questions. Follow this four-part structure:
TITLE: Announce the document type and source. 'The document is an article from [source], published in [year], entitled [title].' If it is a video: 'The document is a video from [channel/website], published in [year], about...'
CONTEXT: Explain the who/what/when/where. 'This article deals with the growing importance of cybersecurity in enterprises.' 'It is about the challenges faced by IT teams when migrating to the cloud.' 'The author describes the evolution of Zero Trust security models.'
MAIN POINTS (present 2-3 key ideas from the document): 'The first main point is that... / Firstly, the author explains that...' 'Secondly/Moreover/Furthermore, the text highlights that...' 'Finally, the author concludes that...'
CONCLUSION: State the author's position and give a brief personal opinion. 'To conclude, the author argues that cloud adoption is inevitable for modern businesses.' 'In my opinion, this is relevant to my work as a future SISR technician because...'

2. CONNECTOR WORDS FOR ORAL USE

Sequencing ideas: firstly, secondly, thirdly, then, next, after that, finally, subsequently. Contrasting: however, on the other hand, although, whereas, nevertheless, despite this. Adding information: moreover, furthermore, in addition, also, besides. Explaining cause: because, since, as, due to the fact that. Expressing result: therefore, as a result, consequently, thus, this means that. Concluding: to conclude, in conclusion, to sum up, all in all, overall.

3. BTS ORAL EXAM TIPS

Speak clearly and at a moderate pace — examiners need to understand every word. Use pauses between ideas — they make your speech more understandable and give you thinking time. Do not apologize for mistakes — simply self-correct and continue. If you forget a word, reformulate: 'What I mean is...' / 'In other words...' / 'That is to say...' Use what you know confidently — better to say something simple and correct than something complex and wrong. Make eye contact with the examiner — do not read from your notes. Practice with a timer: your presentation should last 5-7 minutes.

4. INTRODUCING YOURSELF

'Good morning/afternoon. My name is [First Name] [Last Name]. I am a second-year BTS SIO student specializing in SISR at [School Name] in [City]. Today, I am going to present [document type] about [topic]. This document was published in [year] by [source].'

5. PRESENTING AN IT PROJECT IN ENGLISH

Introduction: 'The context of this project is [company description and IT environment].' 'The main objective was to [describe the IT goal].'
Project description: 'The project took place over [X] weeks. I worked with [team description].' 'The main tasks included [list of tasks].'
Technical details: 'The technologies I used were [list: pfSense, OpenVPN, Cisco switches, etc.].' 'The architecture consisted of [describe network/system architecture].'
Conclusion: 'As a result of this project, [describe outcome].' 'I learned [skills gained].' 'In the future, I would like to improve [further development].'

6. COMMON EXAMINER QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS

'Can you explain what a firewall does?' → 'A firewall is a security device that monitors and filters network traffic based on predefined rules. It can block unauthorized connections and allows only traffic that matches approved rules.'
'What do you mean by network segmentation?' → 'Network segmentation means dividing a network into smaller zones using VLANs or subnets, which improves security by limiting the spread of threats and controlling access between zones.'
'Give an example of a security measure you implemented.' → 'During my internship, I configured firewall rules to restrict traffic between VLANs and set up an OpenVPN server for secure remote access.'
'What are the advantages of virtualization?' → 'Virtualization reduces hardware costs, improves resource utilization, simplifies backup and recovery, and allows faster deployment of new services.'
'How does DNS work?' → 'When a user types a domain name, the DNS resolver sends a query to a DNS server, which returns the corresponding IP address, allowing the browser to connect to the correct server.'

notions_cles.definition: 4-part structure: Title → Context → Main Points → Conclusion
Identifying the main topic from the first few seconds of a document
Firstly / then / finally — used to present ideas in order
However / on the other hand / nevertheless — contrasts two ideas
Moreover / furthermore / in addition — adds a complementary point
'What I mean is...' / 'In other words...' — used to rephrase when stuck
Opening formula: name + BTS SIO SISR + school + today's presentation topic
Context → Tasks → Technologies → Architecture → Results → Lessons learned
Speak clearly, use pauses, do not apologize for mistakes, make eye contact
'The first main point is... / Secondly... / Finally...'
'To conclude, the author argues that... / In my opinion...'
Can you explain? / What do you mean by? / Give an example / What are the advantages?

exemple_concret: Sample oral opening: 'Good morning. My name is Nabil Boudjenah, I am a second-year BTS SIO SISR student at Lycée [Name] in Marseille. Today, I am going to present an article from TechCrunch published in 2024, entitled Cloud Security Challenges in Enterprise Environments. This article deals with the main security risks associated with cloud migration. The first main point is that data breaches are the primary concern. Furthermore, the author highlights the importance of shared responsibility models. To conclude, the author argues that companies must invest in cloud-native security tools. In my opinion, this is highly relevant to SISR technicians who will increasingly work with cloud infrastructure.'

points_cles: Compte rendu: Title → Context → Main Points (2-3) → Conclusion
Start: 'The document is an article from [source], published in [year], entitled [title]'
Main points: 'The first main point is... / Secondly... / Finally...'
Conclusion: 'To conclude... / In my opinion...'
Reformulation when stuck: 'What I mean is...' / 'In other words...'
Do NOT apologize for mistakes — self-correct and continue
Presentation timing: ~5-7 minutes; speak slowly and clearly

cours:
CHAPTER 6 — ADVANCED GRAMMAR (B1-B2)

1. CONDITIONAL SENTENCES

Zero conditional — expresses facts and universal truths (if + present simple, present simple): 'If you press Enter, the command executes.' / 'If the server is overloaded, response times increase.'
First conditional — expresses realistic future situations (if + present simple, will + infinitive): 'If the backup fails, we will lose the data.' / 'If you configure the VLAN correctly, users will have access.'
Second conditional — expresses unlikely or hypothetical situations (if + past simple, would + infinitive): 'If we had more bandwidth, the network would be faster.' / 'If I were the network administrator, I would implement QoS.'
Third conditional — expresses past hypotheticals and regrets (if + past perfect, would have + past participle): 'If we had patched the system in time, the breach would not have occurred.' 'If the administrator had configured the firewall rule correctly, the attack would have been blocked.'

2. PASSIVE VOICE

The passive voice is extensively used in IT documentation because the focus is on the action or the object, not the actor. Formation: subject + be (conjugated) + past participle.
Present passive: 'Data IS STORED on the server.' / 'Packets ARE ROUTED by the router.' / 'IPs ARE ASSIGNED by DHCP.'
Past passive: 'The update WAS APPLIED last night.' / 'The vulnerability WAS DISCOVERED in 2023.' / 'The firewall WAS CONFIGURED by the intern.'
Present perfect passive: 'The patch HAS BEEN DEPLOYED.' / 'The user HAS BEEN NOTIFIED.' / 'All services HAVE BEEN RESTORED.'
Future passive: 'The server WILL BE RESTARTED tonight.' / 'The firmware WILL BE UPDATED during maintenance.'
Modal passive: 'The data SHOULD BE ENCRYPTED.' / 'The system MUST BE PATCHED immediately.' / 'Access CAN BE GRANTED by the admin.'
When to use passive: when the actor is unknown, unimportant, or obvious; in technical documentation; in formal reports.

3. RELATIVE CLAUSES

Relative clauses add information about a noun using a relative pronoun. which/that for things: 'The server WHICH/THAT is in the rack room is overheating.' 'The firewall THAT blocks external traffic needs an update.' who for people: 'The technician WHO configured the switch is on leave.' whose for possession: 'The company WHOSE data was breached issued a statement.' where for places: 'The data centre WHERE our servers are hosted is in Paris.'
Defining relative clauses (no commas) — essential information: 'The switch that connects floor 3 is down.' (tells us which switch — essential). Non-defining relative clauses (with commas) — extra, non-essential information: 'The router, which was installed last year, needs to be replaced.' (the commas indicate this is extra info).'

4. REPORTED SPEECH

Reported speech (indirect speech) shifts the tense back one step. Direct: 'The server is down.' → Reported: 'She said (that) the server was down.' Direct: 'We have applied the patch.' → Reported: 'They said (that) they had applied the patch.' Direct: 'Has the update been deployed?' → Reported: 'He asked whether the update had been deployed.' Direct: 'Restart the service.' → Reported: 'The manager told us to restart the service.'
Tense shifts: present simple → past simple; present continuous → past continuous; present perfect → past perfect; will → would; can → could; must → had to.

5. PERFECT TENSES

Present perfect (have/has + past participle) — for actions with relevance to now: 'I HAVE just configured the VLAN.' (just now) / 'We HAVE deployed the patch.' (result is current) 'The server HAS been down for two hours.' (started in the past, still relevant now) Never use present perfect with a specific past time: NOT 'I have configured it yesterday' → 'I configured it yesterday.'
Past simple — for completed actions at a specific past time: 'I configured it yesterday at 3pm.' / 'They deployed it on Monday.' Present perfect continuous (have/has + been + verb-ing) — for ongoing actions started in the past: 'I HAVE BEEN monitoring the logs all morning.' / 'The server HAS BEEN running hot since the update.'

6. MODALS OF DEDUCTION

must have + past participle (near certainty about past): 'The server must have crashed — all services went down simultaneously.' could have + past participle (past possibility): 'The attack could have been prevented with proper patch management.' might have + past participle (uncertain past possibility): 'The data might have been compromised during the breach.' can't have / couldn't have (impossibility in the past): 'The attacker can't have had administrator credentials — the account was disabled.'

notions_cles.definition: Facts/always true: if + present simple, present simple ('If you reboot, it resets')
Realistic future: if + present simple, will + infinitive ('If it fails, we will lose data')
Hypothetical: if + past simple, would + infinitive ('If we had more RAM, it would be faster')
Past regret: if + past perfect, would have + past participle ('If we had patched it...')
Subject + be + past participle — focuses on the action, not the actor
'The patch HAS BEEN DEPLOYED' — recent action with current relevance
No commas — essential information ('The switch that is broken is on Floor 3')
With commas — extra information ('The router, which was new, failed')
Indirect speech — tense shifts back one step ('She said the server was down')
Have/has + past participle — recent or relevant actions ('I have configured it')
Modal of deduction — near certainty about a past event ('It must have crashed')
Modal of deduction — past possibility ('It could have been prevented')

exemple_concret: Grammar in a real IT report: 'The server HAS BEEN restarted (present perfect passive) following the incident. If the monitoring alert HAD BEEN configured correctly, the team WOULD HAVE detected the issue earlier (third conditional). The vulnerability, WHICH WAS discovered last month (non-defining relative clause), WAS exploited (past passive) because the patch HAD NOT BEEN applied (past perfect passive). The administrator WHO manages the server (defining relative clause) said that he WAS APPLYING the fix (reported speech).'

points_cles: 0 conditional: if + present, present (facts); 1st: if + present, will (realistic future)
2nd conditional: if + past simple, would + inf (hypothetical); 3rd: if + past perfect, would have + pp (regret)
Passive: subject + be(conjugated) + past participle — used in ALL IT documentation
Present perfect (have+pp) = relevance to now; past simple = specific finished time
Defining relative clause (no commas) = essential; non-defining (commas) = extra info
Reported speech: tense shifts back — present → past, will → would, can → could
must have = certainty; could have = possibility; might have = uncertain past deduction
formules: Passive present: The data IS + past participle
Passive past perfect: The patch HAD BEEN + past participle
3rd conditional: If + subject + had + pp, subject + would have + pp
Reported command: [Subject] told us TO + infinitive

cours:
CHAPTER 7 — BTS ORAL SIMULATION (B2)

1. FULL SCRIPT — PRESENTING AN IT INFRASTRUCTURE PROJECT (RP01)

INTRODUCTION:
'Good morning. My name is [Name], I am a second-year BTS SIO student specializing in SISR at [School Name]. Today, I am going to present my first professional situation, which took place at [Company Name], where I completed a [X]-week internship. The title of this project is: Secure Network Infrastructure Deployment.'

CONTEXT:
'The company is a [50]-employee [services sector] company located in [city]. The IT environment consists of approximately [30] workstations, [5] servers, and [10] network devices. The company's main challenge was the absence of network segmentation and the lack of a secure remote access solution for remote workers.'

TECHNICAL DESCRIPTION:
'The project involved designing and implementing a secure network infrastructure. First, I created a VLAN architecture to segment the network into three zones: the server VLAN (VLAN 10), the user VLAN (VLAN 20), and the management VLAN (VLAN 99). Second, I configured a pfSense firewall with appropriate inter-VLAN routing rules to control traffic between zones. Third, I set up an OpenVPN server to allow secure remote access for employees working from home. Finally, I documented all configurations and wrote a deployment report.'

CHALLENGES:
'The main challenge I encountered was configuring the VLAN trunk between the pfSense firewall and the Cisco switch. The 802.1Q tags were not being passed correctly. To solve this, I reviewed the switch configuration, identified a missing trunk encapsulation command, and corrected the configuration.'

CONCLUSION:
'To conclude, this project allowed me to develop practical skills in network segmentation, firewall configuration, and VPN deployment. I learned the importance of thorough documentation and of testing configurations in a lab environment before deploying to production. In the future, I would like to implement network monitoring using Zabbix or Nagios to proactively detect performance anomalies and security incidents.'

2. Q&A PAIRS — LIKELY EXAMINER QUESTIONS

Q: Why did you choose pfSense over a commercial firewall?
A: 'pfSense is an open-source firewall solution based on FreeBSD that offers enterprise-level features at no licensing cost. It supports stateful packet inspection, VPN (OpenVPN and IPsec), VLAN routing, traffic shaping, and intrusion detection. It has an active community and excellent documentation. For a company with a limited IT budget, it represented the best balance of functionality and cost.'

Q: What is a VLAN and why is network segmentation important?
A: 'A VLAN, or Virtual Local Area Network, is a logical network segmentation at Layer 2 that groups devices independently of their physical location. Network segmentation is important for two main reasons: security — it prevents unauthorized lateral movement between network zones, so if a device in the user VLAN is compromised, the attacker cannot directly reach the server VLAN — and performance, because VLANs reduce broadcast domains, which decreases unnecessary network traffic.'

Q: How does OpenVPN work?
A: 'OpenVPN uses TLS encryption to create a secure tunnel between the VPN client and the server. Authentication is performed using X.509 certificates, and optionally a username and password. Once authenticated, all traffic between the client and the corporate network is encrypted using AES-256 and routed through the VPN tunnel. This protects sensitive data transmitted over untrusted public networks such as home broadband or public Wi-Fi.'

Q: What would you improve if you had more time?
A: 'I would implement a network monitoring solution using PRTG or Nagios to proactively detect performance anomalies and receive alerts in real time. I would also add multi-factor authentication for VPN access to strengthen security — currently only certificate-based authentication is used. Additionally, I would implement a syslog server to centralize log collection from all network devices for easier auditing and incident investigation.'

Q: Can you explain the difference between authentication and authorization?
A: 'Authentication is the process of verifying who you are — for example, entering your username and password. Authorization is the process of determining what you are allowed to do once you have been authenticated — for example, a standard user can read files, but only an administrator can modify system settings. In Active Directory, authentication is handled by Kerberos, and authorization is controlled through group memberships and GPOs.'

3. LANGUAGE TIPS FOR THE ORAL EXAM

Use technical vocabulary confidently — examiners appreciate correct use of terms like 'inter-VLAN routing', 'stateful inspection', '802.1Q trunking'. Use the passive voice for technical descriptions: 'VLANs were configured on the switch', 'Traffic is filtered by the firewall'. Use connectors to structure your answer: 'First... Second... Finally...' and 'This means that... As a result...'. If you do not understand a question, ask politely: 'Could you rephrase the question please?' / 'I am sorry, could you repeat that?'

notions_cles.definition: First professional project presented orally during the BTS SIO exam
Open-source FreeBSD-based firewall with enterprise features (VPN, VLAN, IDS)
Open-source VPN solution using TLS/AES encryption for secure remote access
Logical division of a network into isolated zones (server/user/management VLANs)
IEEE standard for carrying multiple VLANs over a single switch port
Routing traffic between VLANs — typically done by a firewall (router-on-a-stick)
Firewall technique that tracks active connections and validates packets against their state
Advanced Encryption Standard with 256-bit key — used in OpenVPN
Typical oral exam questions: explain a concept, give an example, justify a choice
'Could you rephrase the question?' — used when you do not understand
Tools for proactive IT infrastructure monitoring and alerting
Multi-factor authentication added to VPN — certificate + username/password + OTP

exemple_concret: During the oral exam, when asked 'Why did you choose pfSense?', a strong answer demonstrates technical knowledge AND justification: 'pfSense is open-source, which eliminated licensing costs. It supports all required features — VLANs, OpenVPN, firewall rules. The community documentation is excellent. For a 50-person company with a small IT budget, it was the optimal solution compared to commercial alternatives like Fortinet or Palo Alto.'

Introduction: name + BTS SIO SISR + school + project title
Technical: VLAN segmentation (10/20/99) + pfSense firewall + OpenVPN remote access
Challenge: describe problem encountered + solution applied
Conclusion: skills developed + lessons learned + future improvements
Q&A: justify technical choices (open-source, cost, features, community support)
Language: use passive voice for technical descriptions + connectors for structure

Context: 'The company is a [X]-employee [sector] company. The IT environment consists of...'

cours:
CHAPTER 8 — BTS EXAM TECHNIQUE (B2)

1. COMPTE RENDU METHODOLOGY (FULL METHOD)

The compte rendu is the core written exercise of the BTS English exam. It is written in FRENCH and must summarize an English document objectively.

Step 1 — First reading (5 minutes): read the entire article for global understanding. Do not stop at unknown words. Identify: topic, type of document, overall message.
Step 2 — Second reading (10 minutes): read again for details. Identify structure: title, author, source, date. Note the key ideas in each paragraph.
Step 3 — Extract main ideas (5 minutes): identify 3-4 main ideas. These are the backbone of your compte rendu. NOT details (statistics, examples) — the main arguments and conclusions.
Step 4 — Write the compte rendu in FRENCH (15-20 minutes): Paragraph 1 — Introduction: present the document. 'L'article intitulé [title], publié dans [source] en [year], traite de [topic].' 'L'auteur s'intéresse à la question de [main question].'
Paragraph 2 and 3 — Main ideas: present each main idea with connectors. 'Dans un premier temps, l'auteur explique que...' 'Ensuite, il souligne que...' / 'De plus, le texte met en évidence que...' 'En revanche, l'auteur montre que...'
Paragraph 4 — Conclusion: end the compte rendu. 'En conclusion, l'auteur estime que...' / 'L'article suggère en définitive que...'
Length: 180-220 words exactly. Count your words.
CRITICAL RULES: Do NOT copy sentences from the text — always reformulate in your own words. Do NOT add your personal opinion. Do NOT add information not in the text.

2. NOTE DE SYNTHÈSE (MULTIPLE DOCUMENTS)

A note de synthèse synthesizes information from 2-3 documents on the same theme. Key difference from compte rendu: you COMPARE and SYNTHESIZE, not just summarize. Method: identify the common theme, find convergences (points agreed), find divergences (differing views), organize by theme (not by document). Do NOT write: 'Document 1 says... Document 2 says...' — instead, group ideas thematically.

3. B2 WRITTEN EXPRESSION STRUCTURE

The written expression (expression écrite) asks you to write a text of 150-200 words on a given topic. Use this structure:
Introduction (2-3 sentences): contextualize the topic and announce your plan. 'In today's digital world, [topic] has become increasingly important. This email/report will...' Development (2-3 paragraphs): each paragraph contains one main idea + explanation + example + link. 'First of all, [main idea]. This is because [explanation]. For example, [concrete example]. Therefore, [link to question].'
Conclusion (2-3 sentences): synthesize your argument and open to a broader perspective. 'In conclusion, [summary of position]. Looking ahead, [future perspective/recommendation].'

4. USEFUL ACADEMIC PHRASES FOR B2

Presenting information: 'The article highlights that...' / 'According to the author...' / 'The text suggests that...' / 'The author argues that...'
Contrasting: 'On the one hand... on the other hand...' / 'However, it should be noted that...' / 'Despite this, the author maintains that...'
Emphasizing: 'It is worth noting that...' / 'Particularly striking is the fact that...' / 'Above all, the key point is...'
Giving examples: 'This is illustrated by the fact that...' / 'A clear example of this is...' / 'For instance,...'
Concluding: 'To conclude...' / 'All in all...' / 'Ultimately...' / 'In sum...'

5. HANDLING UNKNOWN VOCABULARY

Strategy 1 — Use context: read the full sentence and paragraph. The meaning is often implicit. Strategy 2 — Cognates: look for words resembling French (authentication, configuration, protocol). Strategy 3 — Skip and infer: skip the unknown word, read the rest, then come back and infer its role. Strategy 4 — Do not panic: in a compte rendu, you reformulate anyway — an approximate understanding is sufficient.

6. TIME MANAGEMENT FOR BTS ENGLISH EXAM

Total written exam duration: 2 hours 30 minutes.
20 minutes — Read and annotate the text (first and second reading).
30 minutes — Write the compte rendu in French (180-220 words).
40 minutes — Write the expression écrite in English (150-200 words).
10 minutes — Review: check grammar, spelling, word count, coherence.

Total oral exam: 20-30 minutes (varies by academy).
10 minutes — Preparation time (annotate document, plan oral).
10-15 minutes — Oral presentation (compte rendu oral + project if required).
10 minutes — Examiner questions.

7. MARKING CRITERIA

Comprehension (does your compte rendu reflect the text accurately? — 30%): key ideas present, no misunderstandings, correct identification of main arguments.
Language expression (how well do you write/speak? — 40%): grammar accuracy, vocabulary richness, sentence variety, fluency.
Structure and coherence (is your text/speech organized? — 20%): introduction and conclusion present, logical connectors, paragraph/idea organization.
Task completion (did you do what was asked? — 10%): correct word count, answered the specific question, correct document type.

notions_cles.definition: Summary in French of an English text — objective, no personal opinion, 180-220 words
Synthesis of 2-3 documents — compare and organize by theme, not by document
Written production in English — introduction + development + conclusion, 150-200 words
Expressing the text's ideas in your own words — never copy from the text
Formal expression used in written/oral: 'According to the author...' / 'It is worth noting that...'
2h30 total: 20min read + 30min compte rendu + 40min expression + 10min review
10min prep + 10-15min presentation + 10min Q&A
Marking category: are key ideas correctly identified and presented?
Marking category: grammar, vocabulary, fluency, sentence variety
First quick read to identify topic, type, and general message
'L'article intitulé [titre], publié dans [source] en [année], traite de [sujet].'
Never add personal views in the compte rendu — only what the author says

exemple_concret: BTS exam simulation: given a 300-word article about Zero Trust security. Step 1 (5 min): skim — topic is a new security model. Step 2 (10 min): re-read — main ideas: (1) traditional perimeter security is obsolete, (2) Zero Trust requires continuous verification, (3) micro-segmentation limits breach impact. Step 3 (5 min): plan compte rendu structure. Step 4 (15 min): write in French — 'L'article intitulé Zero Trust: The Future of Enterprise Security, publié dans TechRepublic en 2024, traite des nouveaux modèles de sécurité informatique. Dans un premier temps, l'auteur explique que les approches traditionnelles basées sur le périmètre réseau ne sont plus suffisantes face aux cybermenaces actuelles. Ensuite, il présente le modèle Zero Trust, qui repose sur le principe de ne jamais faire confiance et de toujours vérifier l'identité des utilisateurs et des appareils. De plus, le texte met en évidence l'importance de la micro-segmentation pour limiter la propagation d'une attaque. En conclusion, l'auteur estime que les entreprises doivent adopter une approche Zero Trust pour renforcer leur sécurité dans un environnement de travail hybride.' (195 words — ✓)

points_cles: Compte rendu: in FRENCH, 180-220 words, no personal opinion, no text copies — reformulate
Structure: intro (document presentation) → main ideas (connectors) → conclusion
Compte rendu intro: 'L'article intitulé [titre], publié dans [source] en [année], traite de [sujet].'
Time: 20min read + 30min compte rendu + 40min expression + 10min review = 2h30
Expression écrite: in ENGLISH, 150-200 words, intro + development + conclusion
Note de synthèse: organize by THEME not by document — synthesize, do not juxtapose
Marking: comprehension (30%) + expression (40%) + structure (20%) + task completion (10%)

Main idea connector: 'Dans un premier temps... / Ensuite... / De plus... / En revanche...'
Compte rendu conclusion: 'En conclusion, l'auteur estime que...'
